Skip to content. toolbar 0 Introduction Fundamentally, the importance of mathematics in the modern world comes from our ability to know that mathematical statements are correct. Arguably, one of the mathematical ideas that has had the most impact on the world (and continues to have impact) is that of calculus. Almost every area of science and engineering requires some use of calculus and it has been central to our understanding of the world for at least the last two centuries. However, when we start to delve into calculus, we start to get to some statements which may trip us up (and have tripped up many of the greatest mathematicians of the last two centuries). This can (and has) lead to errors in solutions of equations, mistakes in proofs, entire books that are incorrect… This is where Real Analysis comes in: Real Analysis is the rigorous study of the theory of real numbers and functions on the real numbers. It aims to completely clarify (i.e. prove) the statements that we require as mathematicians. Here are some statements - which of these is true? (Recall that a continuous function is one that “you can draw without taking your pen off the page”) •​ 1=0.99999999999999999999999999999\ldots •​If a,b and c are real numbers and a\leq b then ac\leq bc . •​Every continuous function is differentiable. •​Every differentiable function is continuous. •​Given a sequence of differentiable functions which get closer and closer together, they will limit to a differentiable function. •​There is no continuous curve in the plane (i.e. a curve which you can draw without taking your pen off the page) which covers every point on the plane. •​Suppose that a function f has f^{\prime}(a)>0 . Then, near f the function is strictly increasing. In fact, only the first and fourth bullet points are true and the rest are false! When trying to decide which are true and which are false, you may at some point have felt some uncertainty. Indeed understanding which of these is true starts right at the beginning: What does continuous even really mean? What does it mean for a function to be differentiable? What does 0.9999999999999999… even really mean? So far, you may have been given vague “definitions” such as the “ f^{\prime}(a) is the slope of the graph of f at a ”, or (as above) “ f is continuous if you can draw it without taking the pen off the page”. However, when it comes to actually doing anything at all with these, they are useless! For example we couldn’t either prove or disprove any of the above, which is pretty bad given the importance of calculus. In this course, we will understand more about the real numbers and functions on the real numbers. Starting from just the real numbers, we will see why calculus works and build up the techniques necessary to prove the statements that we need to solve mathematical problems. Beyond this, we will build up proof techniques that will have application throughout your degree. Previous page Next page

Skip to content. toolbar 1 Numbers and inequalities 1.1 Sets of numbers We start with various different types of numbers. We express these as sets, which you will be learning about in more detail in Core Mathematics - essentially a set is a collection of objects, and we can think of a set as a “box” containing mathematical objects. Here are five very important sets which occur regularly in mathematics: \mathbb{Z}^{+} , the set of positive integers: This is the set of “counting” numbers, that is the set of all whole numbers larger than zero, which can be written as \mathbb{Z}^{+}=\{1,2,3,4,\ldots\}\ . Here we have used set notation, the start of the set denoted by \{ and the end of it by \} (so everything in between is in the set).11 1 Some mathematicians call this set \mathbb{N} , but others disagree and say that \mathbb{N} should include 0. To avoid confusion we use \mathbb{Z}^{+} . \mathbb{Z} , the set of integers: This is the set of all whole numbers, including 0 and negative numbers. That is \mathbb{Z}=\{\ldots,-3,-2,-1,0,1,2,3,\ldots\}\ . \mathbb{Q} , the set of rational numbers . This is the set of all numbers that may be written as a fraction of integers. As well as being a set, \mathbb{R} has further structures which have been familiar to you since primary school: \mathbb{R} is a field: The real numbers come with standard operations which are familiar to you, namely addition, subtraction, multiplication and division, that is (+\ -\ 	imes\ \div) , along with the familiar algebraic properties (for example, for any real numbers a,b,c we know that a	imes(b+c)=(a	imes b)+(a	imes c) ). \mathbb{R} is an ordered field: As well as the above we have an ordering relation < . We need to prove the properties that we know about these before using them! 1.2 Modulus and measurement on the real numbers We now define an important function which will be extremely important to us in this course is the modulus function (or absolute value function). This is written as |x| and is defined by |x|:= egin{cases}x&	ext{ if }x\geq 0\ -x&	ext{ if }x<0\end{cases}\ . So |4|=4 , |-2|=-(-2)=2 and so on. Clearly |x|\geq 0 for all x , by definition. If we think of real numbers as lying on an infinite straight line, then |x| can be interpreted geometrically as the distance from x to 0 . Furthermore, given any pair of real numbers x,y , the absolute value of their difference |x-y| can be thought of as the distance between them. Note that |x-y|=|y-x| for all x and y . For example the distance between 4 and -2 is |4-(-2)|=|6|=6\quad\mbox{or}\quad|(-2)-4|=|-6|=-(-6)=6. We emphasize that this is just a mental model of {\mathbb{R}} which gives a nice interpretation of |\cdot| . We are not defining real numbers as points on an infinite line, nor are we defining |x| to be the distance from x to 0 . We will often care about inequalities involving |x| . Lemma 1.1 For any real numbers x and c , |x|<c if and only if -c<x<c . In the above “if and only if” means that |x|<c is true exactly when -c<x<c (and |x|<c is false exactly when -c<x<c is false). As mathematicians are busy people, we often shorten “if and only if” to “iff”. The above seems reasonable – we might draw the following helpful picture: Refer to caption Figure 1: Our intuition for Lemma 1.1. However, drawing a graph helps with intuition, but does not constitute a proof! Proof. Suppose first that |x|<c holds. Note that in this case, 0<c (as 0\leq|x|<c ). We have two cases to consider: •​If x\geq 0 then this implies that 0\leq x=|x|<c so we have that -c<x<c . •​If x<0 , we have -x=|x|<c . Rearranging we have that -c<x . Therefore in this case -c<x<0 which implies -c<x<c . As these are the only two possibilities for x , if |x|<c we always have -c<x<c Now suppose that -c<x<c : Again, this implies that 0\leq c (this follows from rearranging -c<c ). If x\geq 0 then |x|=x<c . If x<0 then |x|=-x<c (where this inequality comes from rearranging -c<x ). Therefore we see that if -c<x<c then |x|<c . ∎ Note that in the above we had to have two steps to prove |x|<c iff -c<x<c . We had to prove both “if |x|<c is true then -c<x<c is true” and “if -c<x<c is true then |x|<c ”. In general, we need to do both! There are lots of true statements of the form “If A then B” is true but “If B then A” is false. For example, “If I am wearing a hat then there is someone in the room that I am in who is wearing a hat” is always true. However, “If there is someone in the room that I am in who is wearing a hat then I am wearing a hat” is false in general. Remark 1.2 There is a second statement which is the weak inequality version (or “ \leq version”) of Lemma 1.1: For any real numbers x and c , |x|\leq c if and only if -c\leq x\leq c . This is also true - the proof is identical to the proof of Lemma 1.1 (with < replaced with \leq ). I leave it as an exercise to check this! Example 1.3 We may see that |x-2|<1 is all numbers x that have -1<x-2<1 or 1<x<3 . We may think of these as all numbers of distance less than 1 from 2. Skip to content. toolbar 10 The Mean Value Theorem 10.1 Maxima and minima Recall (Definition 8.11) that a function f:Dightarrow{\mathbb{R}} attains a maximum at c\in D if, for all x\in D , f(x)\leq f(c) , and attains a minimum at d\in D if, for all x\in D , f(x)\geq f(d) . These definitions don’t make any reference to the derivative of f . You may find this surprising: after all, in your previous studies of calculus, you’ve been trained, when asked to find the maximum or minimum values of a function, to find and examine its critical points (also called “turning” or “stationary” points), that is points x were f^{\prime}(x)=0 . The next theorem66 6 Extremum means “maximum or minimum”. Its plural is “extrema.” explains why. Theorem 10.1 (Interior Extremum Theorem) Let f:(a,b)ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} be differentiable, and attain a maximum or a minimum at c\in(a,b) . Then f^{\prime}(c)=0 . Proof. Assume first that f attains a maximum at c\in(a,b) . Consider the sequences x_{n}=c- rac{(c-a)}{n+1}\qquad\mbox{and}\qquad y_{n}=c+ rac{b-c}{n+1}. These are sequences in (a,b){ ackslash}\{c\} which converge to c , so s_{n}= rac{f(x_{n})-f(c)}{x_{n}-c}ightarrow f^{\prime}(c)\qquad\mbox{and}% \qquad t_{n}= rac{f(y_{n})-f(c)}{y_{n}-c}ightarrow f^{\prime}(c) by the definition of f^{\prime}(c) . But f attains a maximum at c , so f(x_{n})\leq f(c) and f(y_{n})\leq f(c) , and x_{n}<c while y_{n}>c . Hence s_{n}\geq 0 (it’s a non-positive number divided by a negative number) while t_{n}\leq 0 (it’s a non-positive number divided by a positive number). But then \lim_{nightarrow\infty}s_{n}\geq 0\qquad\mbox{and}\qquad\lim_{nightarrow% \infty}t_{n}\leq 0 by Theorem 2.14. Hence f^{\prime}(c)\geq 0 and f^{\prime}(c)\leq 0 , so f^{\prime}(c)=0 . If f attains a minimum at c , we apply the argument above to g(x)=-f(x) , which attains a maximum at c . Then g^{\prime}(c)=0 , so f^{\prime}(c)=-g^{\prime}(c)=-0 . ∎ Example 10.2 Find the maximum and minimum values attained by f:[0,2]ightarrow{\mathbb{R}},\qquad f(x)=x^{3}-x^{2}. Note that f does attain both a maximum and a minimum, by the Extreme Value Theorem (the domain is a closed bounded interval, and the function is continuous). Solution:  Either the extrema occur at the endpoints \{0,2\} or one or both occur in (0,2) . If an extremum occurs at c\in(0,2) then f(c) also an extremum of the function restricted to (0,2) , so f^{\prime}(c)=0 by the Interior Extremum Theorem. Hence, the extrema occur either at an endpoint 0 , 2 , or at some interior critical point, where f^{\prime}(c)=0 . Now f^{\prime}(c)=3c^{2}-2c=c(3c-2)=0 if c=0 or c=2/3 (in this case, one of the critical points is actually also an endpoint). Now f(0)=0,\qquad f(2/3)=- rac{4}{27},\qquad f(2)=4. Hence, the maximum value of f is 4 , attained at the endpoint 2 , and the minimum value is - rac{4}{27} attained at the interior critical point  rac{2}{3} . Note that in this case, the critical point 0 (also an endpoint) is neither a maximum nor a minimum of f . \Box Remark 10.3 Having shown that, for all x\in[0,2] , -4/27=f(2/3)\leq f(x)\leq f(2)=4 , it follows from the Intermediate Value Theorem that the range of this function is f([0,2])=[-4/27,4]. Do you see why? We can combine the Interior Extremum Theorem with the Extreme Value Theorem to give a surprisingly powerful result traditionally called Rolle’s Theorem: Theorem 10.4 (Rolle’s Theorem) Let f be a real function which is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b) , and assume that f(a)=f(b) . Then there exists c\in(a,b) such that f^{\prime}(c)=0 . Proof. By the Extreme Value Theorem, f attains both a maximum and a minimum on [a,b] . If both the maximum and the minimum occur at endpoints of [a,b] , then since f(a)=f(b) , the maximum value equals the minimum value, so f is constant on [a,b] and hence f^{\prime}(c)=0 for all c\in(a,b) , and we’re done. Otherwise, either the maximum or the minimum occurs at an interior point c\in(a,b) . But then f^{\prime}(c)=0 by the Interior Extremum Theorem. ∎ 10.2 Increasing and decreasing functions It is common to interpret the derivative of a function f as the rate of change of f(x) as its input variable x is increased. So, if f^{\prime}(a)>0 , we expect that f is an increasing function, at least for x sufficiently close to a . We’ve already seen that this naive expectation can actually be wrong in general (see Example 9.9). To convert information about the sign of f^{\prime} into information about the increasing or decreasing behaviour of f , we need to be a bit more careful. We start (of course!) by carefully defining our terms. Definition 10.5 A function f:Dightarrow{\mathbb{R}} (where D\subseteq{\mathbb{R}} ) is increasing if, for all x,y\in D , if y>x then f(y)\geq f(x) . It is strictly increasing if, for all x,y\in D , if y>x then f(y)>f(x) . It is decreasing if, for all x,y\in D , if y>x then f(y)\leq f(x) , and strictly decreasing if, for all x,y\in D , if y>x then f(y)<f(x) . Remark Note once again that these definitions have absolutely nothing to do with calculus! They use only the ordering relation on {\mathbb{R}} , and make sense even if the function is not differentiable (or, indeed, continuous). Note also that a function can be both increasing and decreasing – but only if it is constant! (Can you see why?) Example 10.6 The step function f:{\mathbb{R}}ightarrow{\mathbb{R}},\qquad f(x)=\left\{ egin{array}[]{cc}0,% &x<0.\ 1,&x\geq 0.\end{array}ight. is an increasing function. The key result allowing us to deduce information about the decreasing/increasing behaviour of differentiable functions is called the Mean Value Theorem. Then there exists c\in(a,b) such that f^{\prime}(c)= rac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}. Proof. Just apply Theorem 10.7 in the case where g:[a,b]ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} , g(x)=x . ∎ Remarks •​The Mean Value Theorem has a nice geometric interpretation. Consider the graph y=f(x) of a differentiable function f:{\mathbb{R}}ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} . For each pair of distinct numbers a<b , we can construct the chord (straight line) passing through the points (a,f(a)) and (b,f(b)) on the graph. Its slope is (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a) . The Mean Value Theorem asserts that, at some point (c,f(c)) on the graph between these two points, the tangent line to the graph is parallel to the chord. [Uncaptioned image] •​The Mean Value Theorem guarantees the existence of a point c with the required f^{\prime}(c) . It says nothing about uniqueness: there could be more than one such point. [Uncaptioned image] The Mean Value Theorem allows us (among many other things) to relate the sign of f^{\prime} to the increasing/decreasing behaviour of f . Proposition 10.9 Let I be an interval and f:Iightarrow{\mathbb{R}} be differentiable. (a)​If f^{\prime}(x)\geq 0 for all x\in I then f is increasing. (b)​If f^{\prime}(x)>0 for all x\in I then f is strictly increasing. (c)​If f^{\prime}(x)\leq 0 for all x\in I then f is decreasing. (d)​If f^{\prime}(x)<0 for all x\in I then f is strictly decreasing. (e)​ f is constant if and only if f^{\prime}(x)=0 for all x\in I . Proof. The proofs of parts (a)–(d) are similar, so we will do part (a), and leave (b), (c), (d) as an exercise. (a) Assume, towards a contradiction, that f^{\prime}(x)\geq 0 but f is not increasing. Then there exist a,b\in I with a<b such that f(a)>f(b) . But then, by the Mean Value Theorem applied to f on [a,b] , there exists c\in(a,b) such that f^{\prime}(c)= rac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}<0, a contradiction. (e) If f(x)=c , some constant, then f^{\prime}(x)=0 for all x\in I by Example 9.2. Conversely, assume f^{\prime}(x)=0 for all x\in I . Then f is increasing by part (a) and decreasing by part (b), so must be constant. ∎ Remarks (i)​The converses of parts (a) and (c) are also true. That is, if f:Iightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is increasing and differentiable, then f^{\prime}(x)\geq 0 for all x\in I . Similarly if f:Iightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is decreasing and differentiable, then f^{\prime}(x)\leq 0 for all x\in I . One can prove this by adapting the idea of the proof of the Interior Extremum Theorem. (ii)​The converses of parts (b) and (d), by contrast, are false! For example f:{\mathbb{R}}ightarrow{\mathbb{R}},\qquad f(x)=x^{3} is strictly increasing, but its derivative is not strictly positive. (iii)​It’s important to note that Proposition 10.9 concerns functions on intervals. For example f:{\mathbb{R}}{ ackslash}\{0\}ightarrow{\mathbb{R}},\qquad f(x)= rac{1}{x} has f^{\prime}(x)=-1/x^{2} which is negative everywhere, but f(-1)<f(1) , so f is not a decreasing function. This doesn’t contradict Proposition 10.9 since {\mathbb{R}}{ ackslash}\{0\} is not an interval. (iv)​Strictly increasing (or decreasing) functions are always injective, so Proposition 10.9 gives us a sneaky trick to prove that (some) functions are injective. Theorem 10.11 (The Inverse Function Theorem) Let f be continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b) , and assume that, for all x\in(a,b) , f^{\prime}(x)>0 . Then f is a bijective mapping f:[a,b]ightarrow[f(a),f(b)], and its inverse function g:[f(a),f(b)]ightarrow[a,b] is continuous on [f(a),f(b)] , and differentiable on (f(a),f(b)) , with g^{\prime}(y)= rac{1}{f^{\prime}(g(y))}. We conclude that f is actually a bijective map [a,b]ightarrow[f(a),f(b)] and that its inverse function is continuous on [f(a),f(b)] and differentiable on (f(a),f(b)) . The most difficult part of the proof is showing that g is continuous. Proof. I first claim that f is strictly increasing. Assume this is false. Then there exist c,d\in[a,b] such that c<d but f(c)\geq f(d) . But then, by the Mean Value Theorem applied to f on [c,d] , there exists x\in(c,d) such that f^{\prime}(x)= rac{f(d)-f(c)}{d-c}<0, contradicting the fact that f^{\prime} is positive on (a,b) . Since f is strictly increasing, it is injective and bounded below by f(a) and above by f(b) . Further, given any y\in[f(a),f(b)] , there exists x\in[a,b] such that f(x)=y , by the Intermediate Value Theorem, so f is surjective. Hence f is bijective, and it has an inverse function g:[f(a),f(b)]ightarrow[a,b]. Since f is injective, we conclude that c_{*}=c , a contradiction. Hence g(y_{n})ightarrow c , as was to be proved. Finally, we must prove that g is differentiable at every y\in(f(a),f(b)) with the claimed derivative. So, choose y\in(f(a),f(b)) and let (y_{n}) be any sequence in (f(a),f(b)){ ackslash}\{y\} converging to y . We must prove that the sequence of difference quotients s_{n}:= rac{g(y_{n})-g(y)}{y_{n}-y}. converges to 1/f^{\prime}(g(y)) . Since g is bijective, there is a unique c\in(a,b) such that y=f(c) . Let x_{n}:=g(y_{n})\in(a,b) and note that y_{n}=f(x_{n}) , so x_{n}

So (x_{n}) is a sequence in (a,b){ ackslash}\{c\} converging to c , and f is differentiable at c , so  rac{f(x_{n})-f(c)}{x_{n}-c}ightarrow f^{\prime}(c), whence s_{n}= rac{x_{n}-c}{f(x_{n})-f(c)}ightarrow rac{1}{f^{\prime}(c)}= rac{1}% {f^{\prime}(g(y))} by the Algebra of Limits. ∎ Refer to caption Figure 8: To prove that g is continuous at y we must show that, given any sequence y_{n}\in[f(a),f(b)] converging to y=f(c) , the sequence x_{n}=g(y_{n}) converges to c . Remark 10.12 We can easily deduce an equivalent theorem in the case where f^{\prime}(x)<0 for all x\in(a,b) . We just apply Theorem 10.11 to g(x)=-f(x) . Try to formulate this theorem carefully, then prove it. The Inverse Function Theorem allows us to deduce useful facts about lots of functions that are naturally defined as the inverse of some function we already understand, for example, square and other roots. Corollary 10.13 Let p be a fixed positive integer. Given any x\in[0,\infty) , there exists a unique number x^{1/p}\in[0,\infty) , its \mathbf{p}^{\mbox{th}} root, whose p^{\mbox{th}} power is x . The function g:[0,\infty)ightarrow[0,\infty),\qquad g(x)=x^{1/p} is continuous on [0,\infty) and differentiable on (0,\infty) , and its derivative is g^{\prime}(x)= rac{1}{p(x^{1/p})^{p-1}}. Proof. Choose any R>0 and consider the function f(x)=x^{p} on [0,R] . This satisfies the hypotheses of the Inverse Function Theorem (on [a,b]=[0,R] ), so has an inverse function g:[0,R^{p}]ightarrow[0,R] which is continuous on [0,R^{p}] and differentiable on (0,R^{p}) , with derivative g^{\prime}(x)= rac{1}{f^{\prime}(g(x))}. Now g(x)=x^{1/p} by definition, and f^{\prime}(x)=px^{p-1} , so g^{\prime}(x)= rac{1}{p(g(x))^{p-1}}= rac{1}{p(x^{1/p})^{p-1}}. Hence the claims hold on [0,R^{p}] for all R>0 , so hold on [0,\infty) . ∎ 10.4 Higher derivatives and Taylor’s Theorem If a function f:Dightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is differentiable (everywhere on D ) then its derivative defines another function f^{\prime}:Dightarrow{\mathbb{R}} , and we can ask whether this function, in turn, is differentiable. If it is, then its derivative is denoted f^{\prime\prime}:Dightarrow{\mathbb{R}} and called the second derivative of f . Similarly, if f^{\prime\prime}:Dightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is differentiable, its derivative f^{\prime\prime\prime}:Dightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is the third derivative of f . Proceeding inductively, we can define the n^{\mbox{th}} derivative of f , denoted f^{(n)} to be the derivative (if it exists), of f^{(n-1)} , where f^{(0)} is, by definition, just the function f itself (so f^{(1)}=f^{\prime} , f^{(2)}=f^{\prime\prime} etc.). If f^{(n)}:Dightarrow{\mathbb{R}} exists, we say that f is n times differentiable, and if f is n times differentiable for all n\in{\mathbb{Z}}^{+} , we say that f is smooth. Example 10.14 Every polynomial function p:{\mathbb{R}}ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} , p(x)=a_{0}+a_{1}x+\cdots+a_{m}x^{m} , is smooth. This follows immediately from Proposition 9.12. Example 10.15 The function f:{\mathbb{R}}ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} , f(x)=|x|^{3} is twice differentiable but not three times differentiable. To see this, note that f coincides with the smooth function f_{+}(x)=x^{3} on (0,\infty) and with the smooth function f_{-}(x)=-x^{3} on (-\infty,0) , so it suffices to consider the differentiability of f at 0 . Let x_{n} be a sequence in {\mathbb{R}}{ ackslash}\{0\} converging to 0 . Then \left| rac{f(x_{n})-f(0)}{x_{n}-0}ight|=|x_{n}|^{2}ightarrow 0 so f^{\prime}(0)=0 , and it follows that f is once differentiable. In fact f^{\prime}(x)=\left\{ egin{array}[]{cc}f_{+}^{\prime}(x)=3x^{2}&x>0\ 0&x=0\ f_{-}^{\prime}(x)=-3x^{2}&x<0\end{array}ight., so \left| rac{f^{\prime}(x_{n})-f^{\prime}(0)}{x_{n}-0}ight|=|3x_{n}|% ightarrow 0, whence f^{\prime\prime}(0)=0 . Hence, f is twice differentiable, and f^{\prime\prime}(x)=\left\{ egin{array}[]{cc}f_{+}^{\prime\prime}(x)=6x&x>0\ 0&x=0\ f_{-}^{\prime}(x)=-6x&x<0,\end{array}ight\}=6|x|. But f^{\prime\prime} is not differentiable at 0 , by Example 9.6, so f is not three times differentiable. An interesting interpretation of the Mean Value Theorem can be given as follows. Let I\subseteq{\mathbb{R}} be an open interval, f:Iightarrow{\mathbb{R}} be differentiable, and choose and fix some a\in I . Then for any other x\in I , we may apply the Mean Value Theorem to f:[a,x]ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} (if x>a ) or f:[x,a]ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} (if x<a ) to deduce that there exists c between a and x such that f(x)=f(a)+f^{\prime}(c)(x-a). Note that the same equation holds trivially in the case where x=a (with c=x=a ). So, for x close to a (meaning that |x-a| is small) we can approximate f by the constant f(a) , and the error in this approximation is of size |f^{\prime}(c)||x-a| , where c is somewhere between a and x (and, of course, depends on x ). The moral is that differentiability (once) of f on an open interval allows one to approximate f by a degree 0 polynomial function (i.e. a constant), with an error controlled by f^{\prime} . It turns out that if f is n times differentiable one can do better: one can approximate f by a degree (n-1) polynomial function, with an error controlled by f^{(n)} . Theorem 10.16 (Taylor’s Theorem) Let I\subseteq{\mathbb{R}} be an open interval, f:Iightarrow{\mathbb{R}} be (n+1) times differentiable, and a,x\in I with a
eq x . Then there exists c strictly between a and x such that \displaystyle f(x) \displaystyle= \displaystyle f(a)+f^{\prime}(a)(x-a)+ rac{f^{\prime\prime}(a)}{2!}(x-a)^{2}+% \cdots+ rac{f^{(n)}(a)}{n!}(x-a)^{n} \displaystyle\qquad\qquad+ rac{f^{(n+1)}(c)}{(n+1)!}(x-a)^{n+1}. Proof. Choose and fix a,x\in I , x
eq a , and consider the function F:Iightarrow{\mathbb{R}} , F(t)=f(t)+f^{\prime}(t)(x-t)+ rac{f^{\prime\prime}(t)}{2!}(x-t)^{2}+\cdots+%  rac{f^{(n)}(t)}{n!}(x-t)^{n}. Then the claim to be proved is that there exists c between a and x such that (10.11) (10.11) f(x)=F(a)+ rac{f^{(n+1)}(c)}{(n+1)!}(x-a)^{n+1}. Now, by the Product Rule, F is differentiable, and, as is easily verified (exercise!), F^{\prime}(t)= rac{f^{(n+1)}(t)}{n!}(x-t)^{n}. Let G(t)=(x-t)^{n+1} . Then F,G satisfy the hypotheses of the Extended Mean value Theorem (Theorem 10.7) on the interval with endpoints a,x (note that G^{\prime}(t)=0 only if t=x ), so there exists c between a and x such that \displaystyle rac{F^{\prime}(c)}{G^{\prime}(c)} \displaystyle= \displaystyle rac{F(x)-F(a)}{G(x)-G(a)}= rac{f(x)-F(a)}{G(a)} \displaystyle\mbox{i.e.\ }\qquad rac{f^{(n+1)}(c)(x-c)^{n}}{n![-(n+1)(x-c)^{n% }]} \displaystyle= \displaystyle rac{f(x)-F(a)}{(x-a)^{n+1}}, whence (10.11) immediately follows. ∎ The polynomial p_{n}(x)=f(a)+f^{\prime}(a)(x-a)+ rac{f^{\prime\prime}(a)}{2!}(x-a)^{2}+% \cdots+ rac{f^{(n)}(a)}{n!}(x-a)^{n} is often called the {n^{m th}} Taylor approximant of the function f about a , and  rac{f^{(n+1)}(c)}{(n+1)!}(x-a)^{n+1} is called the remainder. Note that in the proof above, F is p_{n} but thought of as a function of the expansions point a rather than the variable x . Example 10.17 Let f:[0,\infty)ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} be the function f(x)=\sqrt{x} . Construct the third Taylor approximant for f about 4 , and hence find an approximation to f(5)=\sqrt{5} . Use Taylor’s Theorem to find upper and lower bounds on \sqrt{5} . Solution:  The first four derivatives of f are \displaystyle f^{\prime}(x) \displaystyle= \displaystyle rac{x^{-1/2}}{2}, \displaystyle f^{\prime\prime}(x) \displaystyle= \displaystyle- rac{x^{-3/2}}{4}, \displaystyle f^{\prime\prime\prime}(x) \displaystyle= \displaystyle rac{3x^{-5/2}}{8}, \displaystyle f^{(4)}(x) \displaystyle= \displaystyle- rac{15x^{-7/2}}{16}, so f(4)=2 , f^{\prime}(4)= rac{1}{4} , f^{\prime\prime}(4)=-2^{-5} , and f^{\prime\prime\prime}(4)=3/2^{8} . It follows that the third Taylor approximant for f about 4 is \displaystyle p_{3}(x) \displaystyle= \displaystyle f(4)+f^{\prime}(4)(x-4)+ rac{f^{\prime\prime}(4)}{2!}(x-4)^{2}+%  rac{f^{\prime\prime\prime}(4)}{3!}(x-4)^{3} \displaystyle= \displaystyle 2+ rac{1}{4}(x-4)- rac{1}{64}(x-4)^{2}+ rac{1}{512}(x-4)^{3}. Using the approximation f(x)\approx p_{3}(x) gives \sqrt{5}=f(5)\approx p_{3}(5)=2+ rac{1}{4}- rac{1}{64}+ rac{1}{512}=2.23632% 8125. By Taylor’s Theorem, the true value of f(5) is (10.12) (10.12) f(5)=p_{2}(5)+ rac{f^{(4)}(c)}{4!}(5-4)^{4}=p_{2}(5)- rac{5}{2^{7}c^{7/2}} for some c\in(4,5) . It follows that the true value of \sqrt{5} is strictly less than p_{2}(5) , but strictly greater than p_{2}(5)-5/(2^{7}4^{7/2}) . That is, we have established that 2.236022949<\sqrt{5}<2.236328125. In fact, we can do slightly better. Noting that c<5 in (10.12), we see that \displaystyle\sqrt{5} \displaystyle< \displaystyle p_{2}(5)- rac{5}{2^{7}5^{7/2}}=p_{2}(5)- rac{\sqrt{5}}{2^{7}5^% {3}} \displaystyle\left(1+ rac{1}{2^{7}5^{3}}ight)\sqrt{5} \displaystyle< \displaystyle p_{2}(5) \displaystyle\sqrt{5} \displaystyle< \displaystyle rac{p_{2}(5)}{1+1/(2^{7}5^{3})}=2.236188363227298. For comparison, my pocket calculator tells me that \sqrt{5}\approx 2.236067977 . \Box Summary •​The Extreme Value Theorem: A continuous function on a closed, bounded interval f:[a,b]ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is bounded above and below, and attains both a maximum and a minimum value. •​The Interior Extremum Theorem: If a differentiable function f(a,b)ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} attains a maximum or a minimum at c\in(a,b) then f^{\prime}(c)=0 . •​The Mean Value Theorem: If a function f is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b) , then there exists c\in(a,b) such that f^{\prime}(c)= rac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}. •​We can use the Mean Value Theorem to show that functions f:Iightarrow{\mathbb{R}} , where I\subseteq{\mathbb{R}} is an interval, are increasing if f^{\prime}(x)\geq 0 for all x\in I (and decreasing if f^{\prime}(x)\leq 0 for all x\in I ). •​The definitions of increasing, decreasing, maximum and minimum have nothing to do with calculus. But we can use calculus to find/understand them. •​A function f:Dightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is n times differentiable if all its derivatives up to the n^{m th} exists everywhere in D . It is smooth if it is n times differentiable for all n\in{\mathbb{Z}}^{+} . •​A function which is (n+1) times differentiable can be approximated by a polynomial of degree n , with an error controlled by f^{(n+1)} , its (n+1)^{m th} derivative. This follows from Taylor’s Theorem. Previous page Next page

Skip to content. toolbar 11 Differentiability of power series 11.1 Power series define smooth functions Recall that a power series is a series of the form \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}a_{n}x^{n} where x is a real variable and a_{0},a_{1},a_{2},\ldots is a real sequence (often called the coefficients of the power series). So this is actually a collection of infinitely many series, one for each choice of value for x . As usual, the series converges at a particular fixed value of x if its sequence of partial sums \displaystyle s_{0} \displaystyle= \displaystyle a_{0} \displaystyle s_{1} \displaystyle= \displaystyle a_{0}+a_{1}x \displaystyle s_{2} \displaystyle= \displaystyle a_{0}+a_{1}x+a_{2}x^{2} \displaystyle\vdots converges in the sense of Definition 2.4, and converges absolutely if the associated series \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}|a_{n}x^{n}| converges. Certainly this sequence converges if x=0 , since it is the constant sequence (a_{0},a_{0},a_{0},\ldots) , but it may diverge for other values of x . The set of values x\in{\mathbb{R}} for which the series converges is almost completely determined by its radius of convergence (Definition 5.28): R=\sup\{|x|\in[0,\infty):\mbox{$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}|a_{n}x^{n}|$ converges}\}. This follows from Theorem 5.30: the series converges absolutely for all |x|<R and diverges for all |x|>R . So, a power series with radius of convergence R defines a function f:(-R,R)\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}},\quad f(x)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}a_{n}x^{n}. Switching viewpoint, its partial sums can be considered to be a sequence of polynomial functions: \displaystyle s_{0}(x) \displaystyle= \displaystyle a_{0} \displaystyle s_{1}(x) \displaystyle= \displaystyle a_{0}+a_{1}x \displaystyle s_{2}(x) \displaystyle= \displaystyle a_{0}+a_{1}x+a_{2}x^{2} \displaystyle\vdots Each of these is certainly a differentiable function (on the whole of {\mathbb{R}} ), so one might expect that their limit, f(x)=\lim_{k\rightarrow\infty}s_{k}(x) , is also a differentiable function, and that f^{\prime}(x)=\lim_{k\rightarrow\infty}s_{k}^{\prime}(x)=\lim_{k\rightarrow% \infty}a_{1}+x_{2}x+\cdots ka_{k}x^{k-1}=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}na_{n}x^{n-1}. This turns out to be true, at least on (-R,R) where R is the radius of convergence of f , but proving it is no simple matter. The problem is that f^{\prime}(x) is really a double limit f^{\prime}(x)=\lim_{y\rightarrow x}\frac{f(y)-f(x)}{y-x}=\lim_{y\rightarrow x}% \lim_{k\rightarrow\infty}\sum_{n=0}^{k}a_{n}\frac{y^{n}-x^{n}}{y-x}, and our expected formula for f^{\prime}(x) results from computing the double limit in the opposite order \lim_{k\rightarrow\infty}\lim_{y\rightarrow x}\sum_{n=0}^{k}a_{n}\frac{y^{n}-x% ^{n}}{y-x}. But swapping the order in which we evaluate a double limit can, in general, change its value! Example 11.1 Consider the sequence of functions s_{k}:{\mathbb{R}}\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}},\qquad s_{k}(x)=\frac{kx^{2}-1}{kx^{% 2}+1}. Let’s compare the double limits \lim_{x\rightarrow 0}\lim_{k\rightarrow\infty}s_{k}(x)\quad\mbox{and}\quad\lim% _{k\rightarrow\infty}\lim_{x\rightarrow 0}s_{k}(x). We first note that, for all x\neq 0 , the real sequence s_{k}(x)=\frac{x^{2}-1/k}{x^{2}+1/k}\rightarrow\frac{x^{2}}{x^{2}}=1 by the Algebra of Limits. On the other hand, if x=0 , s_{k}(0)=\frac{0-1}{0+1}=-1\rightarrow-1. So \lim_{k\rightarrow\infty}s_{k}(x)=S(x)=\left\{\begin{array}[]{cc}1,&x\neq 0,\\ -1,&x=0.\end{array}\right. Hence \lim_{x\rightarrow 0}\lim_{k\rightarrow\infty}s_{k}(x)=\lim_{x\rightarrow 0}S(% x)=1. Turning to the second double limit, for each k\in{\mathbb{Z}}^{+} , \lim_{x\rightarrow 0}s_{k}(x)=s_{k}(0)=-1, since the function s_{k} is continuous. Hence \lim_{k\rightarrow\infty}\lim_{x\rightarrow 0}s_{k}(x)=\lim_{k\rightarrow% \infty}-1=-1. The two limits are different! \Box So computing \lim_{k\rightarrow\infty}\lim_{y\rightarrow x}\sum_{n=0}^{k}a_{n}\frac{y^{n}-x% ^{n}}{y-x} in principle tells us precisely nothing about \lim_{y\rightarrow x}\lim_{k\rightarrow\infty}\sum_{n=0}^{k}a_{n}\frac{y^{n}-x% ^{n}}{y-x}. We will need to work considerably harder! We begin by showing that a power series and the obvious candidate for its derivative have the same radius of convergence. Lemma 11.2 The power series \displaystyle{f(x)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}a_{n}x^{n}} and \displaystyle{g(x)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}na_{n}x^{n-1}} have the same radius of convergence. Proof. We define the following subsets of {\mathbb{R}} , A=\{|x|\>:\>\mbox{$\sum|a_{n}x^{n}|$ converges}\},\qquad B=\{|x|\>:\>\mbox{$% \sum|na_{n}x^{n-1}|$ converges}\}, and recall that \sup A and \sup B are the radii of convergence of f and g , respectively. We will prove that (i)​for all x with |x|<\sup B , f(x) converges absolutely (so |x|\in A , and hence \sup A\geq\sup B ), and (ii)​for all x with |x|<\sup A , g(x) converges absolutely (so |x|\in B , and hence \sup B\geq\sup A ). It follows immediately that \sup A=\sup B , which is what we seek to prove. (i) Let x\in{\mathbb{R}} with |x|<\sup B . Then s_{k}=\sum_{n=1}^{k}|na_{n}x^{n-1}| converges (Theorem 5.30), and hence is bounded above, and t_{k}=\sum_{n=0}^{k}|a_{n}x^{n}|=|a_{0}|+|x|\sum_{n=1}^{k}|a_{n}x^{n-1}|\leq|a% _{0}|+|x|s_{k}. So (t_{k}) is increasing and bounded above, and hence converges, by the Monotone Convergence Theorem. Hence, |x|\in A . (ii) Let x\in{\mathbb{R}} with |x|<\sup A . If x=0 then g(x) certainly converges, so we may assume |x|>0 . Choose \rho\in(|x|,\sup A) . Then t_{k}=\sum_{n=0}^{k}|a_{n}|\rho^{n} converges (Theorem 5.30), and hence is bounded above. Now s_{k}=\sum_{n=1}^{k}|na_{n}x^{n-1}|=|x|^{-1}\sum_{n=1}^{k}n\left(\frac{|x|}{% \rho}\right)^{n}|a_{n}|\rho^{n}. We have seen that the sequence n(|x|/\rho)^{n}\rightarrow 0 , since (|x|/\rho)<1 (Example 5.16), so it must be bounded, that is, there exists K>0 such that, for all n , n(|x|/\rho)^{n}\leq K . But then s_{k}\leq|x|^{-1}\sum_{n=0}^{k}K|a_{n}|\rho^{n}=\frac{K}{|x|}t_{k}. Hence, s_{k} is increasing and bounded above, so converges, by the Monotone Convergence Theorem (Corollary 3.20). Hence, |x|\in B . ∎ We now show that g(x) really is the derivative of f(x) . Theorem 11.3 Suppose that \displaystyle{f(x)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}a_{n}x^{n}} has radius of convergence R>0 . Then f is differentiable on (-R,R) , and f^{\prime}(x)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}na_{n}x^{n-1}. Proof. Choose and fix y\in(-R,R) . Let g(x)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}na_{n}x^{n-1},\quad\mbox{and}\quad h(x)=\sum_{n=2}^{% \infty}n(n-1)a_{n}x^{n-2}. By Lemma 11.2, g(x) has radius of convergence R , so by Lemma 11.2 again (applied to g(x) ), h(x) also has radius of convergence R . Now choose and fix some \rho\in(|y|,R) , for example \rho=(|y|+R)/2 will do. Then, by Theorem 5.30, h(\rho) converges absolutely, meaning that the sequence of partial sums \sum_{n=2}^{k}n(n-1)|a_{n}|\rho^{n-2} converges. But convergent sequences are bounded, so there exists C>0 such that, for all k\in{\mathbb{Z}}^{+} , (11.13) (11.13) \sum_{n=2}^{k}n(n-1)|a_{n}|\rho^{n-2}\leq C. We wish to show that \lim_{x\rightarrow y}\frac{f(x)-f(y)}{x-y}=g(y), that is, for all sequences x_{j}\in(-R,R){\backslash}\{y\} such that x_{j}\rightarrow y , s_{j}:=\frac{f(x_{j})-f(y)}{x_{j}-y}-g(y)\rightarrow 0. So, let (x_{j}) be any such sequence and denote by s_{j} the corresponding sequence defined above. Since x_{j}\rightarrow y and |y|<\rho , there exists J\in{\mathbb{Z}}^{+} such that, for all j\geq J , |x_{j}-y|<\rho-|y| , and hence |x_{j}|<\rho . By the Algebra of Limits, for each fixed j\in{\mathbb{Z}}^{+} , s_{j}=\lim_{k\rightarrow\infty}P_{jk} where P_{jk}:=\sum_{n=0}^{k}a_{n}\left\{\frac{x_{j}^{n}-y^{n}}{x_{j}-y}-ny^{n-1}% \right\}. Note that the n=0 and n=1 terms in this sum are identically 0 , so actually P_{jk}=\sum_{n=2}^{k}a_{n}\left\{\frac{x_{j}^{n}-y^{n}}{x_{j}-y}-ny^{n-1}% \right\}. Let us consider P_{jk} for some fixed choice of j,k\in{\mathbb{Z}}^{+} with j\geq J . We will make repeated use of the identity (11.14) (11.14) \alpha^{n}-\beta^{n}=(\alpha-\beta)(\alpha^{n-1}+\alpha^{n-2}\beta+\alpha^{n-3% }\beta^{2}+\cdots+\alpha^{2}\beta^{n-3}+\alpha\beta^{n-2}+\beta^{n-1}), which may be proved by induction on n\in{\mathbb{Z}}^{+} (exercise!). Using this to factorize x_{j}^{n}-y^{n} and cancel the factors of x_{j}-y , we see that \displaystyle P_{jk} \displaystyle=\sum_{n=2}^{k}a_{n}\left\{x_{j}^{n-1}+x_{j}^{n-2}y+x_{j}^{n-3}y^% {2}+\cdots x_{j}y^{n-2}+y^{n-1}-ny^{n-1}\right\} \displaystyle=\sum_{n=2}^{k}a_{n}\left\{[x_{j}^{n-1}-y^{n-1}]+[x_{j}^{n-2}y-y^% {n-1}]+[x_{j}^{n-3}y^{2}-y^{n-1}]+\cdots+[x_{j}y^{n-2}-y^{n-1}]\right\} \displaystyle=\sum_{n=2}^{k}a_{n}(x_{j}-y)\left\{\right. \displaystyle\begin{array}[]{llllcl}\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad x_{j}^{n-2}&+x_{j% }^{n-3}y&+x_{j}^{n-4}y^{2}&+&\cdots&+y^{n-2}\\ &+x_{j}^{n-3}y&+x_{j}^{n-4}y^{2}&+&\cdots&+y^{n-2}\\ &&+x_{j}^{n-4}y^{2}&+&\cdots&+y^{n-2}\\ &&&&\vdots&\\ &&&&&+y^{n-2}\left.\right\}\end{array} \displaystyle=(x_{j}-y)\sum_{n=2}^{k}a_{n}\left\{x_{j}^{n-2}+2x_{j}^{n-3}y+3x_% {j}^{n-4}y^{2}+\cdots+(n-1)y^{n-2}\right\} Now |y|<\rho and |x_{j}|<\rho for all j\geq J , so \displaystyle|P_{jk}| \displaystyle\leq \displaystyle|x_{j}-y|\sum_{n=2}^{k}|a_{n}|\left\{\rho^{n-2}+2\rho^{n-2}+3\rho% ^{n-2}+\cdots+(n-1)\rho^{n-2}\right\} \displaystyle= \displaystyle\frac{1}{2}|x_{j}-y|\sum_{n=2}^{k}n(n-1)|a_{n}|\rho^{n-2} where we have use the formula 1+2+3+\cdots N=\frac{1}{2}N(N+1) in the case N=n-1 . Hence, by (11.13), we know that for all k\in{\mathbb{Z}}^{+} and all j\geq J |P_{jk}|\leq\frac{1}{2}C|x_{j}-y| Recall that s_{j}=\lim_{k\rightarrow\infty}P_{jk} . Applying Proposition 2.14 to the sequence P_{jk} indexed by k , with j\geq J fixed, we see that, for all j\geq J , |s_{j}|\leq\frac{1}{2}C|x_{j}-y|. But x_{j}\rightarrow y , so |x_{j}-y|\rightarrow 0 and hence, by the Squeeze Rule (and the Tail Lemma) s_{j}\rightarrow 0 . This completes the proof. ∎ So the derivative of a power series exists on its open interval of convergence and is just the power series obtained by termwise differentiation. Since f^{\prime}(x) is also a power series with radius of convergence R , we can apply Theorem 11.3 to f^{\prime}(x) and deduce that f:(-R,R)\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is actually twice differentiable. Further, f^{\prime\prime}(x) also has radius of convergence R , so is differentiable on (-R,R) , that is, f is three times differentiable. In fact, we can keep applying Theorem 11.3 as often as we like, and we conclude that f is smooth. Corollary 11.4 Let f(x)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}a_{n}x^{n} have radius convergence R>0 . Then f:(-R,R)\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is a smooth function, and a_{n}=\frac{f^{(n)}(0)}{n!}. Proof. For each integer k\geq 0 , define g_{k}(x)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{(n+k)!}{n!}a_{n+k}x^{n}. I claim that each power series g_{k}(x) has radius of convergence R and that, for all x\in(-R,R) , f^{(k)}(x)=g_{k}(x) . We prove this by induction on k . Certainly the claim holds for k=0 , since g_{0}=f . So, assume that the claim holds for some value k\geq 0 , and consider g_{k+1} . Defining coefficients b_{n}=\frac{(n+k)!}{n!}a_{n+k} so that g_{k}(x)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}b_{n}x^{n} , then \displaystyle g_{k+1}(x) \displaystyle= \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{(n+k+1)!}{n!}a_{n+k+1}x^{n}=\sum_{m=1}^{% \infty}\frac{(m+k)!}{(m-1)!}a_{m+k}x^{m-1} \displaystyle= \displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}mb_{m}x^{m-1}. Hence, by Lemma 11.2, g_{k+1} has the same radius of convergence as g_{k} , and by Theorem 11.3, g_{k+1}(x) coincides with the derivative of g_{k} . But, by our induction hypothesis, g_{k} has radius of convergence R and coincides with f^{(k)} , so g_{k+1} has radius of convergence R and coincides with f^{(k+1)} . Hence, if the claim holds for some k\geq 0 , it also holds for k+1 . Hence, by induction, the claim holds for all integers k\geq 0 . It follows that f^{(k)}(0)=g_{k}(0)=k!a_{k} , which completes the proof. ∎ Corollary 11.4 applies to any power series with non-zero radius of convergence so, in particular, it applies to the series defining \exp , \sin , and \cos . Proposition 11.5 The functions \exp:{\mathbb{R}}\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}} , \sin:{\mathbb{R}}\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}} , \cos:{\mathbb{R}}\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}} are smooth, and their derivatives are \exp^{\prime}=\exp,\qquad\sin^{\prime}=\cos,\qquad\cos^{\prime}=-\sin. Proof. That these functions are smooth follows immediately from Corollary 11.4. Furthermore, by Theorem 11.3 \exp^{\prime}(x)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}n\left(\frac{1}{n!}\right)x^{n-1}=\sum_{n=% 1}^{\infty}\frac{x^{n-1}}{(n-1)!}=\sum_{m=0}^{\infty}\frac{x^{m}}{m!}=\exp(x) for all x\in{\mathbb{R}} , so \exp^{\prime}=\exp . The formulae for \sin^{\prime} and \cos^{\prime} follow from similar arguments. ∎ Smooth functions are differentiable, and hence continuous, so this restrospectively justifies our assumption that \sin:{\mathbb{R}}\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}} (for example) is continuous. 11.2 Exponentials and logarithms Now that we know that \exp is differentiable, and its derivative is \exp , we can prove that it has all the nice properties you’re familiar with. Proposition 11.6 For all x,y\in{\mathbb{R}} , \exp(x+y)=\exp(x)\exp(y) . Proof. Choose any constant a\in{\mathbb{R}} and consider the function f:{\mathbb{R}}\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}},\qquad f(x)=\exp(x)\exp(a-x). By the Product and Chain Rules, this is differentiable with derivative f^{\prime}(x)=\exp^{\prime}(x)\exp(a-x)+\exp(x)(-\exp^{\prime}(a-x))=\exp(x)% \exp(a-x)-\exp(x)\exp(a-x)=0. Hence by Proposition 10.9, f is constant, that is, for all x\in{\mathbb{R}} , f(x)=f(a) , that is \exp(x)\exp(a-x)=\exp(a)\exp(0)=\exp(a). This holds for all a\in{\mathbb{R}} and, in the case a=x+y , yields the claimed identity. ∎ Proposition 11.7 \exp is a bijective map {\mathbb{R}}\rightarrow(0,\infty) . Proof. We first note that there is no x\in{\mathbb{R}} such that \exp(x)=0 since, if there were, then Proposition 11.6 with y=-x would imply 1=\exp(0)=\exp(x-x)=\exp(x)\exp(-x)=0\times\exp(-x)=0. Since \exp is continuous and \exp(0)=1>0 , it follows that \exp(x)>0 for all x\in{\mathbb{R}} , by the Intermediate Value Theorem (if \exp(x)<0 there would exist c between 0 and x with \exp(c)=0 , contradicting the fact just established). Hence, \exp^{\prime}(x)=\exp(x)>0 for all x\in{\mathbb{R}} , so \exp is strictly increasing by Proposition 10.9, and hence is injective. It remains to prove that \exp is surjective, that is, its range, \exp({\mathbb{R}}) , is (0,\infty) . Now \exp(0)=1 so 1\in\exp({\mathbb{R}}) . By the Mean Value Theorem, for each x>0 there exists c\in(0,x) such that \displaystyle\frac{\exp(x)-\exp(0)}{x-0} \displaystyle= \displaystyle\exp^{\prime}(c)=\exp(c) \displaystyle\Rightarrow\qquad\exp(x) \displaystyle= \displaystyle 1+x\exp(c)\geq 1+x\exp(0)=1+x since, as we have observed, \exp is increasing. Hence \exp is unbounded above. So for each y>1 , there exists a\in{\mathbb{R}} such that \exp(a)>y . But then, by the Intermediate Value Theorem, there exists x\in[0,a] such that \exp(x)=y . Hence every y>1 is in \exp({\mathbb{R}}) . Finally, given any y\in(0,1) , 1/y>1 so, as we just showed, there exists x\in{\mathbb{R}} such that \exp(x)=1/y . But then \exp(-x)=1/(1/y)=y by Proposition 11.6, so y\in\exp({\mathbb{R}}) also. ∎ Since \exp is a bijective function {\mathbb{R}}\rightarrow(0,\infty) it has an inverse function (0,\infty)\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}} which is conventionally called the natural logarithm \ln . Proposition 11.8 The natural logarithm function \ln:(0,\infty)\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is differentiable and \ln^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{x}. Proof. Given any \rho>0 , \exp is continuous on [-\rho,\rho] , differentiable on (-\rho,\rho) and has strictly positive derivative. Hence, by the Inverse Function Theorem (Theorem 10.11), its inverse function \ln:[\exp(-\rho),\exp(\rho)]\rightarrow[-\rho,\rho] exists and is differentiable, with derivative \ln^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{\exp^{\prime}(\ln(x))}=\frac{1}{\exp(\ln(x))}=\frac{1% }{x}. Since this holds for all \rho>0 , the claim is established. ∎ So, for each positive number y , \ln y is the real number whose exponential is y . For example, \exp(0)=1 (just evaluate the power series defining exp(x) at x=0 ) and so \ln 1=0 . Every property of \exp has a counterpart for \ln . In particular, the identity \exp(x+y)\equiv\exp(x)\exp(y) is equivalent to Proposition 11.9 For all x,y\in(0,\infty) , \ln(xy)=\ln x+\ln y . Proof. Choose and fix y\in(0,\infty) and consider the function f:(0,\infty)\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}},\qquad f(x)=\ln(xy)-\ln x-\ln y. By the Chain Rule and Proposition 11.8, f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{y}{xy}-\frac{1}{x}=0 so f is constant (Proposition 10.9). Hence, for all x , f(x)=f(1)=\ln y-0-\ln y=0 . ∎ Exercise 11.10 Prove Proposition 11.9 by using Proposition 11.6 directly. A second useful identity quickly follows from this. Proposition 11.11 For all x\in(0,\infty) and n\in{\mathbb{Z}} , \ln x^{n}=n\ln x . Proof. Exercise. (Hint: proof by induction.) ∎ Recall we defined Euler’s number to be e=\exp(1)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n!}. It follows that \ln e=1 . There is an alternative way to define e , as the limit of the sequence (1+\frac{1}{n})^{n} . We are now in a position to prove this. Proposition 11.12 The sequence \displaystyle{x_{n}=\left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)^{n}} converges to e . Proof. It is clear that x_{n}>0 for all n . Now, for all n , by Proposition 11.11, \displaystyle\ln x_{n} \displaystyle= \displaystyle n\ln\left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right) \displaystyle= \displaystyle\frac{\ln\left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)-\ln 1}{\left(1+\frac{1}{n}% \right)-1} \displaystyle= \displaystyle\frac{\ln y_{n}-\ln 1}{y_{n}-1} where y_{n}=1+\frac{1}{n} . Note that y_{n}\rightarrow 1 . Since \ln is differentiable (everywhere, and, in particular, at 1 ), the sequence of difference quotients \frac{\ln y_{n}-\ln 1}{y_{n}-1} converges to \ln^{\prime}(1)=1/1=1 , that is, \ln x_{n}\rightarrow 1. Now \exp is continuous (everywhere, and, in particular, at 1 ), so \exp(\ln x_{n})\rightarrow\exp(1)=e. But \exp(\ln x_{n})=x_{n} by definition of \ln , so x_{n}\rightarrow e . ∎ 11.3 Exactly summing (some) series We finish this section with a nice trick. Sometimes we can exactly sum a series by thinking of it as a power series evaluated at a specific value x . Here’s a simple example: Example 11.13 Exactly sum \displaystyle{\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{n}{2^{n}}} . Solution:  It’s easy to show (using the Ratio Test, for example) that the series converges, but this doesn’t tell us what its limit is. We need to be sneakier. Consider the power series f(x)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}x^{n}. This is the Geometric Series with common ratio x . We showed previously that it has radius of convergence R=1 , and for all x\in(-1,1) , its limit is (11.15) (11.15) f(x)=\frac{1}{1-x}. By Theorem 11.3, this function is differentiable on (-1,1) and f^{\prime}(x)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}nx^{n-1}. But, by the Chain Rule, we already know from (11.15) that f is differentiable with derivative f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{(1-x)^{2}}. Hence, for all x\in(-1,1) , \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}nx^{n-1}=\frac{1}{(1-x)^{2}}. In the special case x=1/2 , we deduce that \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{n}{2^{n-1}}=\frac{1}{(1/2)^{2}}=4, and hence \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{n}{2^{n}}=\frac{1}{2}\times 4=2. \Box Summary •​A power series f(x)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}a_{n}x^{n} with radius of convergence R>0 is differentiable on (-R,R) , and its derivative is f^{\prime}(x)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}na_{n}x^{n-1}. This power series also has radius of convergence R . •​It follows that f:(-R,R)\rightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is actually smooth (infinitely differentiable), and that a_{n}=\frac{f^{(n)}(0)}{n!}. •​These facts apply to all convergent power series, including those defining \exp , \sin and \cos . Skip to content. toolbar 3 Sets of real numbers You will have studied sets in Core Mathematics over the last few weeks, and we now remind ourselves of some set notation. •​Recall that a set is a collection of objects, real or of the imagination. •​Given a set A , we write x\in A to denote that object x is in set A . This is usually translated into words (when reading the expression) as “ x is an element of A ”. The expression x
otin A means x is not an element of A . •​Another set B is a subset of A if every element of B is an element of A . This is denoted B\subseteq A . Note that every set is a subset of itself, A\subseteq A . Two sets A , B are equal if they have precisely the same elements, in which case A\subseteq B and B\subseteq A . •​We have already seen several sets of numbers such as the real numbers \mathbb{R} , the positive integers \mathbb{Z}^{+} , the integers \mathbb{Z} and the rational numbers (or quotients) \mathbb{Q} (see section 1.1). •​A further very important set if the empty set, denoted \emptyset . This is the set containing no objects. Do not confuse it with \{\emptyset\} , which certainly isn’t the empty set since it contains an object (namely, the empty set)! One of the most common ways of specifying a set is by simply listing its elements inside curly brackets, for example A=\{1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8\}\ . A second if by giving a condition which elements must satisfy, A=\{x\>:\>x\in{\mathbb{Z}}^{+},\,x\leq 8\}. In general A=\{x\>:\>P(x)\} means the set of objects x for which condition P(x) is true. If part of condition P(x) is that x should be in a particular set, we often move that part to the left of the colon. For example, the set above could be written A=\{x\in{\mathbb{Z}}^{+}\>:\>x\leq 8\} and we could write the rational numbers as \mathbb{Q}=\left\{x\in\mathbb{R}:x= rac{a}{b}	ext{ where }a,b\in\mathbb{Z},b
