For example, the negation of the statement “if n is prime then n is odd” is the statement “ n is prime and n is not odd”, not the statement “if n is not prime then n is not odd.” 6.3 Quantifiers We have been a little bit sloppy up to now in dealing with statements which contain variables. Consider, for example, the statement x>2. Is this really a statement? Until one specifies a particular value for x , one cannot say whether it is true. So it’s true for x=3 , and false for x=-2 , for example. What about if x=1+3i ? The “statement” 1+3i>2 is pretty meaningless, and it isn’t very helpful to declare it either true or false (e.g. if you decide it’s false, then you’d expect 1+3i\leq 2 to be true, and this “statement” is equally meaningless). If we’re being careful, we should make explicit the condition that x is a real number, and if we denote the statement x>2 with a symbol, we should choose one like P(x) , rather than P , to remind ourselves that whether P(x) is true depends on the particular x under consideration. So P(3) is true, in this case, while P(2) is false. There are two very useful ways to turn a statement with a variable into an unambiguous true-or-false statement, using quantifiers. Let P(x) be any statement which makes sense for all values of x in some specified set A (e.g.  A={\mathbb{R}} and P(x):x>2 , as above). Then  orall x\,P(x) means “for all x\in A , P(x) is true” and \exists x\,P(x) means “there exists some x\in A such that P(x) is true”. The symbol  orall is called the universal quantifier and the symbol \exists is called the existential quantifier. For the example above,  orall x\,P(x) is false, because, for example, P(-1) is false (since -1 is not greater than 2 ), but \exists x\,P(x) is true because, for example, P(3) is true. Note that, once we’ve coupled it with a quantifier, the statement no longer contains a variable, and is a simple true or false assertion. Note also that it’s entirely irrelevant what we call the variable. So  orall y\,P(y) is precisely the same statement as  orall x\,P(x) . In practice, rather than specifying the set A in advance, we often specify it explicitly with the quantifier. In the above example, we would write  orall x\in{\mathbb{R}},\,x>2 which is false, and (6.9) (6.9) \exists x\in{\mathbb{R}},\,x>2 which is true. You should feel free to add punctuation and/or ordinary English to expressions like this, if you think it clarifies the meaning. For example, I think \exists x\in{\mathbb{R}}\mbox{ such that }x>2 is clearer than (6.9). Indeed, you should feel free to avoid using the symbols  orall and \exists entirely if you think they obscure the mathematical meaning of the sentences you are writing. As we will see, they can be extremely useful, but you shouldn’t chuck them into your mathematical prose willy-nilly. They won’t make your proofs any more rigorous, elegant or impressive. Example 6.6 Translate the following sentences into symbols: (i)​The square of any even integer is even. In symbols:  orall n\in{\mathbb{Z}},[(n/2)\in{\mathbb{Z}}\Rightarrow(n^{2}/2)\in{\mathbb{% Z}}] (ii)​Given any real number K there is a positive integer N such that N>K . In symbols:  orall K\in{\mathbb{R}},\,\exists N\in{\mathbb{Z}}^{+}\,N>K (iii)​There exists a rational number whose square is 2. In symbols: \exists x\in{\mathbb{Q}},\,x^{2}=2 (iv)​There exists a real number which is bigger than all rational numbers. In symbols: \exists x\in{\mathbb{R}},\, orall y\in{\mathbb{Q}}\,x>y (v)​The sequence (a_{n}) converges to L . In symbols:  orall\varepsilon\in(0,\infty),\,\exists N\in{\mathbb{Z}}^{+},\, orall n\in{% \mathbb{Z}}^{+}\cap[N,\infty),\,|a_{n}-L|<\varepsilon (vi)​The sequence (a_{n}) converges. In symbols: \exists L\in{\mathbb{R}},\, orall\varepsilon\in(0,\infty),\,\exists N\in{% \mathbb{Z}}^{+},\, orall n\in{\mathbb{Z}}^{+}\cap[N,\infty),\,|a_{n}-L|<\varepsilon Example 6.7 Translate the following “sentences” into English: (i)​ \displaystyle{ orall x\in{\mathbb{Z}},\,\,x^{2}>x} Translation: The square of every integer x exceeds x . Better translation: Every integer is less than its square. (ii)​ \displaystyle{\exists x\in{\mathbb{R}},\,\, orall y\in{\mathbb{R}}\,\,x^{2}>y} Translation: There exists a real number whose square is greater than every real number. (iii)​ \displaystyle{ orall y\in{\mathbb{R}},\,\,\exists x\in{\mathbb{R}}\,\,x^{2}>y} Translation: For each real number y , there exists a real number x whose square exceeds y . Better translation: The function f:{\mathbb{R}}ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} , f(x)=x^{2} , is unbounded above. In the above example, we had a statement depending on two variables, P(x,y) , to which we applied a pair of quantifiers, \exists x and  orall y . An important point to notice is that it matters which order we apply the quantifiers in! That is \exists x\,\, orall y\,\,P(x,y)\qquad\mbox{is not the same as}\qquad orall y% \,\,\exists x\,\,P(x,y). Skip to content. toolbar 7 Limits of functions 7.1 Cluster points and limits of functions We now have a very precise (and powerful) definition of limit for real sequences. In this section we will show how to use this to give a precise definition of the limit of a real function of a real variable. We will also see that basic properties of limits of functions, which are usually asserted without proof in introductory calculus courses, follow very easily from the corresponding limit theorems for sequences, which we proved in section 2.2. Informally, \lim_{xightarrow a}f(x)=L means that f(x) is “arbitrarily close” to L for all x “sufficiently close to, but different from” a . So limits concern the behaviour of x arbitrarily close to a , but not at a . There is no reason why a has to be in the domain of f , that is, f(a) may, or may not, be defined. If it is, its value should be irrelevant to \lim_{xightarrow a}f(x) . For example, f(x)= rac{\sin x}{x} is clearly undefined at 0 (its domain is (-\infty,0)\cup(0,\infty) ), but \lim_{xightarrow 0}f(x) is well defined (it’s 1 ). In order to probe the behaviour of f(x) for values of x close to, but different from, a , we can consider the sequence f(x_{n}) where (x_{n}) is any sequence converging to a with x_{n}
eq a . From now on, given a set D\subseteq\mathbb{R} we will say that a sequence a_{n} is in D if for every n\in\mathbb{Z}^{+} , a_{n}\in D . This leads us to the main definition of this chapter: Definition 7.1 Let D\subseteq{\mathbb{R}} and f:Dightarrow{\mathbb{R}} . Then f has limit L at a if, for all sequences (x_{n}) in D{ ackslash}\{a\} such that x_{n}ightarrow a , f(x_{n})ightarrow L . In this case, we usually denote the number L by \lim_{xightarrow a}f(x) . It’s important to note that the definition says that for all sequences (x_{n}) in D{ ackslash}\{a\} converging to a , f(x_{n})ightarrow L . It’s not enough to show that f(x_{n})ightarrow L for just one specific sequence. Note that the sequence (x_{n}) gets arbitrarily close to a (since x_{n}ightarrow a ), but never equals a (since it takes values in D{ ackslash}\{a\} ). So the value of f at a , if this exists, is irrelevant. In order for Definition 7.1 to make sense, there must exist at least one sequence in D{ ackslash}\{a\} such that x_{n}ightarrow a . One reason to prefer our sequential approach to limits is that, having defined limits purely in terms of convergence of sequences, it is very easy to establish many of their basic properties, since these follow directly from theorems about sequences which we’ve already proved. Hey! You now know what limits are! It’s worthwhile to pause and reflect on what we’ve achieved in the first semester. We now have a completely precise and rigorous definition of \lim_{xightarrow a}f(x) for functions f:Dightarrow{\mathbb{R}} , where D is a subset of {\mathbb{R}} . It follows, that we now know, with no hand-waving or weasel words, precisely what the limit f^{\prime}(a)=\lim_{xightarrow a} rac{f(x)-f(a)}{x-a} means, that is, we have put the definition of the derivative of a function on a proper mathematical footing. Note that this is not a limit of f:Dightarrow{\mathbb{R}} , but of the associated function h:D{ ackslash}\{a\}ightarrow{\mathbb{R}},\qquad h(x)= rac{f(x)-f(a)}{x-a}. The interesting, and somewhat curious, thing about this is that we managed to do this by studying sequences and, in particular, making precise what it means for sequences to converge. So from the rather modest and unassuming definition of convergence of sequences (Definition 2.4) something very large and powerful has grown. Summary •​Let f:Dightarrow{\mathbb{R}} where D\subseteq{\mathbb{R}} . We say that f has limit L at a , written \lim_{xightarrow a}f(x)=L , if for all sequences (x_{n}) in D{ ackslash}\{a\} which converge to a , f(x_{n}) converges to L . •​This definition only makes sense if a is a cluster point of D , that is, a point for which there exists at least one sequence in D{ ackslash}\{a\} converging to a . •​A subset D of {\mathbb{R}} may contain points which are not cluster points of D . It may also have cluster points which are not in D . •​The Algebra of Limits for functions follows immediately from the Algebra of Limits for sequences: \displaystyle\lim_{xightarrow a}(f(x)+g(x)) \displaystyle= \displaystyle\lim_{xightarrow a}f(x)+\lim_{xightarrow a}g(x), \displaystyle\lim_{xightarrow a}f(x)g(x) \displaystyle= \displaystyle[\lim_{xightarrow a}f(x)][\lim_{xightarrow a}g(x)], \displaystyle\lim_{xightarrow a} rac{f(x)}{g(x)} \displaystyle= \displaystyle rac{\lim_{xightarrow a}f(x)}{\lim_{xightarrow a}g(x)}, under suitable assumptions. Skip to content. toolbar 8 Continuous functions In this second half of the module, we will take all the theory about convergence of sequences (and series) we’ve developed in Semester 1, and use it to develop a precise and rigorous theory of calculus (both differential and integral) of real-valued functions of a single real variable. Since we now have a precise definition of limit (see Definition 7.1) we’re already halfway there: we know precisely what we mean by the derivative f^{\prime}(a) of a differentiable function f . (Of course, just defining the derivative isn’t the end of the story: we also want to prove that derivatives have all the properties you know and love, that they obey the Product and Chain rules and so on.) Before embarking on that endeavour we will retrench a little, however. Instead of diving straight into differentiability, it will turn out to be important to thoroughly understand a more rudimentary property of (some) functions: continuity. 8.1 Sequential continuity Given a function f:{\mathbb{R}}ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} , what does it mean to say that the function is continuous? It’s common, when first introduced to the notion of continuity, to be told that a function is continuous “if you can draw its graph without taking your pencil off the paper”. Clearly this is not an acceptable mathematical definition: what if you have no pencil, or paper, or hands? Does the continuity or otherwise of f depend on your skill as a draughtsman? More importantly, can you imagine ever being able to prove statements about continuous functions starting from such a “definition”? So our first job will be to define continuity precisely. In fact it’s quite easy to give a precise (and rather elegant) definition of continuity using nothing more than the notion of convergence of sequences. The definition works equally well when the domain of f is a subset of {\mathbb{R}} , not necessarily the whole of {\mathbb{R}} , so that is how we will formulate it. Given a set D\subseteq\mathbb{R} we will say that a sequence a_{n} is in D if for every n\in\mathbb{Z}^{+} , a_{n}\in D . Definition 8.1 Let D\subseteq{\mathbb{R}} . A function f:Dightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is continuous at a point a\in D if, for all sequences (x_{n}) in D such that x_{n}ightarrow a , f(x_{n})ightarrow f(a) . If f is not continuous at a\in D , we say that f is discontinuous at a . The function is continuous if it is continuous at a for all a\in D . So what is this definition saying? Roughly speaking, this is saying that for points that get arbitrarily close to a\in D , the images of these points under f get arbitrarily close to f(a) . Refer to caption Figure 4: In this picture f is continuous at a since, for any sequence a_{n} converging to a , f(a_{n}) converges to f(a) . But f is discontinuous at b since there exist sequences b_{n} converging to b such that f(b_{n}) does not converge to f(b) . One such sequence is depicted. In particular they are always continuous: Proposition 8.4 Every polynomial function p:{\mathbb{R}}ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is continuous. Proof. We prove this by induction on m , the degree of the polynomial p . So, if m=0 , then p(x) is a degree 0 polynomial, that is, p(x)=a_{0} , constant. Hence p(x) is continuous (see Example 8.2). Now assume that every polynomial of degree k is continuous. Let p(x) be a degree k+1 polynomial. Then p(x)=xq(x)+a_{0} where q(x) is a polynomial of degree k and so is, by assumption, continuous. Now f(x)=x is continuous (see Example 8.2), so p(x)=xq(x)+a_{0} is continuous by Theorem 8.3(i),(ii). Hence, by induction every polynomial is continuous. ∎ A nice thing about the set of polynomials is that it is closed under function composition. That is, if p and q are polynomials, so is q\circ p (recall that (q\circ p)(x)=q(p(x)) ). For example: \displaystyle p(x) \displaystyle= \displaystyle x^{3}+1,\qquad q(x)=3x^{2}-2 \displaystyle\Rightarrow(q\circ p)(x) \displaystyle= \displaystyle q(x^{3}+1) \displaystyle= \displaystyle q(x^{3}+1) \displaystyle= \displaystyle 3(x^{6}+2x^{3}+1)-2 \displaystyle= \displaystyle 3x^{6}+6x^{3}+1. So given a pair of polynomials (certainly continuous), their composition is a polynomial (and hence is continuous). Is this a special feature of polynomials? No: it follows almost immediately from our definition of continuity that the composition of two continuous functions is continuous. Theorem 8.5 Let D,E\subseteq{\mathbb{R}} , f:Dightarrow E and g:Eightarrow{\mathbb{R}} . If f is continuous at a\in D and g is continuous at f(a)\in E , then g\circ f is continuous at a . Proof. Let (x_{n}) be any sequence in D which converges to a . Then f(x_{n})ightarrow f(a) (since f is continuous at a ), so (g\circ f)(x_{n})=g(f(x_{n}))ightarrow g(f(a))=(g\circ f)(a) (since g is continuous at f(a) ). Hence g\circ f is continuous at a . ∎ 8.3 The Intermediate Value Theorem To say that this theorem is “important” would be something of an understatement. It is the basis of a useful method for approximately solving equations (the bisection method) and, as we will see, implies the existence of square roots and other similar functions. Theorem 8.6 (Intermediate Value Theorem) Let f:[a,b]ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} be continuous, and y be any real number between f(a) and f(b) . Then there exists c\in[a,b] such that f(c)=y . Refer to caption Figure 5: A picture of the Intermediate Value Theorem in the case f(a)<f(b) . Here we see that for y\in[f(a),f(b)] we have a c\in[a,b] with f(c)=y . Proof. There are three possibilities: (i) f(a)=f(b) , (ii) f(a)<f(b) and (iii) f(a)>f(b) . (i) If f(a)=f(b) then the only real number between them is y=f(a) , so c=a will do. (ii) Assume f(a)<f(b) . If f(b)=y then c=b will do. So assume y<f(b) . Define the set A=\{x\in[a,b]\>:\>f(x)\leq y\}, which is nonempty (since a\in A ) and bounded above (by b ). Hence by Axiom 3.11, there exists c , the supremum of A . We will prove that c\in[a,b] and that f(c)=y , which completes case (ii). c is an upper bound on A and a\in A , so a\leq c . Further, b is also an upper bound on A , and c is the least upper bound on A , so c\leq b . Hence c\in[a,b] . Consider c-1/n , where n\in{\mathbb{Z}}^{+} . Since this is less than c , it is not an upper bound on A , so there exists x_{n}\in A with x_{n}>c-1/n . But then c-1/n\leq x_{n}\leq c , so x_{n}ightarrow c by the Squeeze Rule. f is continuous at c , so f(x_{n})ightarrow f(c) . But each x_{n}\in A , so f(x_{n})\leq y for all n , and hence its limit cannot exceed y , by Proposition 2.14. Hence f(c)\leq y . Hence c\in A and c
eq b , so c<b . Consider now the sequence z_{n}=c+(b-c)/n . This is a sequence in [a,b] each term of which exceeds c , so for all n , z_{n}
otin A (recall c is an upper bound on A ). Hence f(z_{n})\geq y for all n . But z_{n}ightarrow c and f is continuous, so f(z_{n})ightarrow f(c) . Hence, by Proposition 2.14, f(c)\geq y . Since we’ve already shown that f(c)\leq y , it follows that f(c)=y . (iii) If f(a)>f(b) , define \widetilde{f}:[a,b]ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} such that \widetilde{f}(x)=-f(x) and \widetilde{y}=-y . Then \widetilde{f} is continuous, \widetilde{f}(a)<\widetilde{f}(b) , and \widetilde{y} is between \widetilde{f}(a) and \widetilde{f}(b) , so, as proved in part (ii), there exists c\in[a,b] such that \widetilde{f}(c)=\widetilde{y} . But then f(c)=y . ∎ Note that the assumption that the domain of f is an interval is crucial: Example 8.7 f:{\mathbb{R}}{ ackslash}\{0\}ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} , f(x)=1/x is continuous (by Example 8.2). Let a=-1 and b=1 . Then f(a)=-1 and f(b)=1 and so 0 is a number between f(a) and f(b) . But there is no c such that f(c)=0 ( 1/c=0 has no solution). This is not a counterexample to Theorem 8.6, since f is not continuous on [-1,1] . Refer to caption Figure 6: f:{\mathbb{R}}{ ackslash}\{0\}ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} , f(x)=1/x , is continuous, and 0 is a number between f(-1) and f(1) . But there is no number c\in[-1,1] such that f(c)=0 . Example 8.8 Let f:{\mathbb{R}}ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} such that f(x)=x^{2}-3 . Then f is continuous. In particular, it is continuous on [0,2] . Now f(0)=-3 and f(2)=1 , so 0 is a number between f(0) and f(2) . Hence, by the Intermediate Value Theorem, there exists c\in[0,2] such that f(c)=0 . That is, there exists c\in[0,2] such that c^{2}=3 . Note that the Intermediate Value Theorem only tells us that c exists, not what it is, or whether it is unique (maybe there are hundreds of different c ’s!). However, by repeatedly applying the theorem, we can find an approximate solution to f(c)=0 to arbitrarily high accuracy. We know that f(0)<0 and f(2)>0 , so 0 is a number between f(0) and f(2) , and hence, there is some c\in[0,2] such that f(c)=0 . Now test the midpoint of [0,2] : \displaystyle f(1) \displaystyle= \displaystyle 1-3<0\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad So 0 is a number between f(1) and f(2) , and f is continuous on [1,2] , so by the IVT, there exists c\in[1,2] such that f(c)=0 . (Had f(1) been positive, we’d have deduced that c\in[0,1] . Had it been 0 , we’d have deduced that c=1 exactly!) Now test the midpoint of [1,2] , and so on: \displaystyle f(1.5) \displaystyle= \displaystyle-0.75<0\qquad\Rightarrow\qquad c\in[1.5,2] \displaystyle f(1.75) \displaystyle= \displaystyle 0.0625>0\qquad\Rightarrow\qquad c\in[1.5,1.75] \displaystyle f(1.625) \displaystyle= \displaystyle-0.359375<0\qquad\Rightarrow\qquad c\in[1.625,1.75] This is called the “bisection method”. Each time we test the midpoint and apply the IVT, we halve the width of the interval in which we’ve “trapped” our solution c . We initially know c is in [0,2] an interval of width 2 , so after n iterations of the method, we know that c is in some interval of width 2	imes(1/2)^{n}=1/2^{n-1} (check: after n=4 iterations, we should have c trapped in an interval of width 1/8 — correct!). Let’s say we need to know c to within an error of 5	imes 10^{-5} . Then we need to trap it in an interval of width less than 0.00005 . How many iterations of the method do we need? The answer is any n for which  rac{1}{2^{n-1}}<0.00005. will do. We know that such an n exists because the sequence 1/2^{n-1} converges to 0 . If we allow ourselves to use logarithms, we can solve the above inequality. Otherwise, trial and error (substituting values of n ) shows that n=16 is the smallest positive integer satisfying the inequality. So, not only will the bisection method give us the (approximate) solution to the accuracy we require, we can figure out before we start how many iterations we’ll need to make to get this accuracy. By the way, we’ve just proved that \sqrt{3} exists! We can easily generalize the argument above to show that every x\geq 0 has a square root \sqrt{x}\geq 0 , and that this number is unique. Proposition/Definition 8.9 For each x\in[0,\infty) there exists a unique y\in[0,\infty) such that y^{2}=x . We denote this number \sqrt{x} and call it the square root of x . Proof. Consider the function f:[0,\infty)ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} , f(y)=y^{2}-x, noting that it is polynomial and hence continuous. Then f(0)=-x\leq 0 and f(x+ rac{1}{2})=\left(x+ rac{1}{2}ight)^{2}-x=x^{2}+ rac{1}{4}\geq rac{1% }{4}, so 0 is a number between f(0) and f(x+ rac{1}{2}) . Hence, by the Intermediate Value Theorem, there exists y\in[0,x+ rac{1}{2}] such that f(y)=0 . Then y^{2}=x , by the definition of f , so \sqrt{x} exists. It remains to prove that it is unique. Let z\in[0,\infty) satisfy f(z)=0 also. Then 0=f(y)-f(z)=y^{2}-z^{2}=(y-z)(y+z), so z=y or z=-y . But since z\geq 0 and y\geq 0 , z=-y implies z=0=y . So f(z)=f(y) implies that z=y . Hence the square root of x is unique. ∎ We just proved that there exists a function f:[0,\infty)ightarrow[0,\infty),\qquad f(x)=\sqrt{x}. What properties does it have? Is it injective? Surjective? Continuous? Can you prove your answers? 8.4 Maxima, minima and the Extreme Value Theorem We have seen that continuous functions on closed bounded intervals [a,b] have the very useful and fundamental “intermediate value” property: if they attain two values y_{1} and y_{2} , they attain all values between y_{1} and y_{2} . In this section we will prove they have another fundamental property: they are bounded (above and below), and attain both a maximum and a minimum value. Before proceeding further, we need to make all these terms precise. Recall that the range of a function f:Dightarrow E is the subset f(D)=\{f(x)\in E:x\in D\} of E . Definition 8.10 A function f:Dightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is bounded above if its range f(D)\subseteq{\mathbb{R}} is bounded above, that is, if there exists L\in{\mathbb{R}} such that, for all x\in D , f(x)\leq L . We call any such L\in{\mathbb{R}} an upper bound on f . Similarly, f is bounded below if f(D)\subseteq{\mathbb{R}} is bounded below, that is, if there exists K\in{\mathbb{R}} such that, for all x\in D , f(x)\geq K . We call any such K\in{\mathbb{R}} a lower bound on f . The function f is bounded if it is bounded above and below. [Uncaptioned image] [Uncaptioned image] A bounded function. K is a lower bound and L is an upper bound. An unbounded function. This has a lower bound (for example K ) but no upper bound. Definition 8.11 A function f:Dightarrow{\mathbb{R}} attains a maximum at c\in D if, for all x\in D , f(x)\leq f(c) . It attains a minimum at c\in D if, for all x\in D , f(x)\geq f(c) . Remark Note that these definitions have absolutely nothing to do with calculus! In fact, they make sense even if D is not a subset of {\mathbb{R}} . For example, D could be the set of books on my bookshelf, and f could be the map which assigns to each book x the number of pages it contains. Clearly this function will attain both a maximum and a minimum (my bookshelf will contain both a longest and shortest book), but it’s absurd to try to define its derivative. Note also that, if a function attains a maximum, at c say, it is certainly bounded above, by f(c) . Similarly, if it attains a minimum at b , it is bounded below by f(b) . But it’s quite possible for a function to be bounded above but not attain a maximum, or bounded below and not attain a minimum. For example f:(0,\infty)ightarrow{\mathbb{R}},\qquad f(x)= rac{1}{x} is bounded below, by 0 for example, but does not attain a minimum: whatever c\in(0,\infty) we try, there is always some other point x\in(0,\infty) with f(x)<f(c) : we could take x=c+1 , for example. If f is bounded above, then its range f(D) is a nonempty subset of {\mathbb{R}} that is bounded above which, by the Axiom of Completeness, has a supremum. We often refer to this number as the supremum of the function f . Similarly, if f is bounded below, we define its infimum to tbe the infimum of its range. Finally, note that if f attains a maximum at c , then its supremum is precisely f(c) : for all x\in D , f(x)\leq f(c) , so f(c) is an upper bound on f , and any number K<f(c) is not an upper bound on f , since f(c)>K ! Similarly, if f attains a minimum at b then its infimum is f(b) . We may now prove our next big theorem: Theorem 8.12 (Extreme Value Theorem) Let f:[a,b]ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} be continuous. Then f is bounded and attains both a maximum and a minimum. Proof. We first prove that f is bounded above. Assume, towards a contradiction that f is not bounded above. Then every n\in{\mathbb{Z}}^{+} is not an upper bound on f , so there exists x_{n}\in[a,b] such that f(x_{n})>n . But (x_{n}) is a bounded sequence (above by b , below by a ), so has a convergent subsequence (x_{n_{k}}) by the Bolzano-Weiertsrass Theorem. Since a\leq x_{n_{k}}\leq b , its limit c , say, is also in [a,b] . Since x_{n_{k}}ightarrow c\in[a,b] and f is continuous (at c , in particular), f(x_{n_{k}})ightarrow f(c) . Since f(x_{n_{k}}) converges, it is bounded above. But f(x_{n_{k}})>n_{k}\geq k , a contradiction. Hence, f is bounded above. We now prove that f attains its supremum. Since the set A=\{f(x):x\in[a,b]\} is bounded above, and is nonempty, it has a supremum L , say, by the Axiom of Completeness. Now, for each n\in{\mathbb{Z}}^{+} , L-1/n<L , so is not an upper bound on A . Hence, for each n\in{\mathbb{Z}}^{+} , there exists y_{n}\in[a,b] such that f(y_{n})>L-1/n . Clearly f(y_{n})\leq L so, by the Squeeze Rule, f(y_{n})ightarrow L . Since (y_{n}) is bounded, it too has a convergent subsequence y_{m_{k}} say, converging to some limit d\in[a,b] , by the Bolzano-Weiertsrass Theorem. Since f is continuous at d , f(y_{m_{k}})ightarrow f(d) . But f(y_{m_{k}})ightarrow L , since it is a subsequence of f(y_{n}) . Limits are unique, so f(d)=L=\sup A . Hence, for all x\in[a,b] , f(x)\leq f(d) , that is, f attains a maximum (at d ). It follows immediately that f attains a minimum also: let g:[a,b]ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} , g(x)=-f(x) . This is continuous so, by the work above, attains a maximum at some point d\in[a,b] . But then f attains a minimum at d . ∎ Note that all the hypotheses are important for this theorem. If f:[a,b]ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is not continuous, it may fail to be bounded: f:[0,1]ightarrow{\mathbb{R}},\qquad f(x)=\left\{ egin{array}[]{cc} rac{1}{% x},&x\in(0,1]\ 0,&x=0,\end{array}ight. or be bounded but fail to attain a maximum or minimum: f:[0,1]ightarrow{\mathbb{R}},\qquad f(x)=\left\{ egin{array}[]{cc}x,&x\in(0% ,1)\ 1/2,&x\in\{0,1\}.\end{array}ight. Similarly, if the domain is not a closed bounded interval, the conclusion may fail, even if f is continuous: f:(0,1)ightarrow{\mathbb{R}},\qquad f(x)= rac{1}{x} is unbounded, and f:(0,1)ightarrow{\mathbb{R}},\qquad f(x)=x is bounded but attains neither a maximum nor a minimum. These examples are depicted below: [Uncaptioned image] (a)​A function f:[0,1]ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} that is unbounded above. Note that f is discontinuous. (b)​A bounded function f:[0,1]ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} that attains neither a maximum nor a minimum value. Again, note that f is discontinuous. Again, note that (0,1) is not closed. 8.5 An equivalent definition of continuity We have defined continuity using convergence of sequences: f:Dightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is continuous at a\in D if, for all sequences (x_{n}) in D that converge to a , f(x_{n})ightarrow f(a) . Many mathematicians call this property “sequential continuity”, and define continuity at a\in D in a rather different way, by means of what is known as an “ \varepsilon – \delta criterion.” Definition 8.13 (The \epsilon – \delta definition of continuity) A function f:Dightarrow\mathbb{R} is \epsilon – \delta continuous at a point a\in D if for each \epsilon>0 there exists \delta>0 so that, for all x\in D , if |x-a|<\delta then |f(x)-f(a)|<\epsilon\ . [Uncaptioned image] It turns out that these two ways to define continuity are exactly equivalent. Theorem 8.14 A function f:Dightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is continuous at a\in D if and only it is \epsilon – \delta continuous at a . Proof. Assume f is \epsilon – \delta continuous at a\in D , and let (x_{n}) be any sequence in D such that x_{n}ightarrow a . We must show that f(x_{n})ightarrow f(a) , so f is continuous at a . We will do this by formulating a direct \varepsilon – N proof of convergence. So, let \varepsilon\in(0,\infty) be given. Since f is \epsilon – \delta continuous at a , there exists \delta>0 such that, for all x\in D with |x-a|<\delta , |f(x)-f(a)|<\varepsilon . Choose such a number \delta>0 . Since x_{n}ightarrow a , by definition there exists N\in{\mathbb{Z}}^{+} such that, for all n\geq N , |x_{n}-a|<\delta . But then, for all n\geq N , x_{n}\in D and |x_{n}-a|<\delta , so f(x_{n})-f(a)|<\varepsilon (recall we chose \delta specifically to ensure this was true!). Hence f(x_{n})ightarrow f(a) , as was to be proved. Conversely, assume that f is not \varepsilon – \delta continuous at a\in D . Then there exists some \varepsilon_{*}>0 such that, for all \delta>0 , there is some x\in D with |x-a|<\delta such that |f(x)-f(a)|>\varepsilon_{*} . Such an x\in D exists for each \delta>0 so, in particular, for each \delta=1/n , where n\in{\mathbb{Z}}^{+} , there exists x_{n}\in D such that |x_{n}-a|<1/n but |f(x_{n})-f(a)|>\varepsilon_{*} . Clearly x_{n}ightarrow a (by the Squeeze Rule) but, equally clearly f(x_{n})
rightarrow f(a) (it fails the \varepsilon – N criterion for convergence when \varepsilon=\varepsilon_{*} ). Hence f is not continuous at a . ∎ Exercise 8.15 Prove that the function f:{\mathbb{R}}ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} , f(x)=x^{2} is \epsilon – \delta continuous everywhere on \mathbb{R} . Is this easier, or harder, than proving that it’s continuous using our original (sequential) definition of continuity? Summary •​A function f:Dightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is continuous at a\in D if, given any sequence (x_{n}) in D converging to a , f(x_{n}) converges to f(a) . •​A function f:Dightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is continuous if it is continuous at every point a\in D . •​Sums, products, quotients and compositions of continuous functions are continuous. It follows that all polynomials and rational functions (quotients of polynomials) are continuous. •​A second equivalent definition of continuity is the following: f:Dightarrow\mathbb{R} is continuous at a point a\in D if for any \epsilon>0 there is a \delta>0 so that for any x\in D , if |x-a|<\delta then |f(x)-f(a)|<\epsilon . •​The Intermediate Value Theorem: If f:[a,b]ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is continuous and y\in{\mathbb{R}} is a number between f(a) and f(b) then there exists c\in[a,b] such that f(c)=y . •​We can use the Intermediate Value Theorem to show that square (and other) roots exist. In fact, for any p\in\mathbb{Z}^{+} , the function f(x)=x^{1/p} is continuous on [0,\infty) . •​We can also use Intermediate value theorem to see that every cubic polynomial has at least one root. •​The Extreme Value Theorem: If f:[a,b]ightarrow{\mathbb{R}} is continuous, then f is bounded and attains both a maximum and a minimum value. •​Many mathematicians define continuity differently, using an \varepsilon – \delta criterion. This definition is exactly equivalent to ours. Previous page Next page
